items.prompt_md: Why does the contains-duplicate solution use a `set` instead of a `dict`?
options: Sets are always faster than dicts in Python
The problem only needs a yes/no membership check, with no extra value to store per key
A set can hold duplicate values while a dict cannot
Sets preserve insertion order and dicts do not
items.answer: The problem only needs a yes/no membership check, with no extra value to store per key
items.explanation_md: A dict pairs each key with a value you'll look up later (like an index or count). Here the answer is entirely "have I seen this?" — a set is a hash map with no attached value, which is exactly what's needed.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the one-pass set solution to Contains Duplicate, for an array of length n?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: One scan of the array, with each set `in` check and insert averaging O(1), gives O(n) time overall and O(n) extra space — versus O(n log n) for a sort-based check or O(n^2) for pairwise comparison.

items.prompt_md: What question should you ask yourself to decide whether a `set` is enough, instead of a `dict`?
options: "Is the input already sorted?"
"If I answer 'seen it' or 'not seen it,' am I done?"
"Is the input larger than 1000 elements?"
"Does the language support hash maps?"
items.answer: "If I answer 'seen it' or 'not seen it,' am I done?"
items.explanation_md: If the problem never needs to look anything up *by* the value afterward — no index, no paired data — existence alone answers the question, and a set is the leaner tool.

items.prompt_md: In the longest-consecutive-sequence trick, why do you only start counting a run at a number `x` whose predecessor `x - 1` is **absent** from the set?
options: Because starting only at true run-starts means each number gets counted as part of exactly one run, keeping the total work linear
Because it makes the set smaller
Because Python sets are ordered and this preserves that order
Because it avoids using the `in` operator
items.answer: Because starting only at true run-starts means each number gets counted as part of exactly one run, keeping the total work linear
items.explanation_md: Every number in the array is only ever "walked" once — as part of the single run it belongs to, starting from that run's true beginning — so total work across all runs is O(n), not O(n) starts each doing O(n) work.

items.prompt_md: In the longest-consecutive-sequence problem, why does starting a count-up from *every* number in the set (not just run-starts) make the algorithm slower?
options: It doesn't — it's exactly as fast
Because a number in the middle of a long run re-walks the same run every time it's used as a start, so long runs get scanned once per member — O(n^2) worst case
Because it uses more memory than a dict would
Because sets don't support the `in` operator inside a loop
items.answer: Because a number in the middle of a long run re-walks the same run every time it's used as a start, so long runs get scanned once per member — O(n^2) worst case
items.explanation_md: A run of length k, walked from every one of its k members, costs O(k) each time — O(k^2) total for that run alone. Skipping non-boundary starts is what collapses this back to O(n).

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the longest-consecutive-sequence algorithm when it only starts counting from numbers whose predecessor is absent from the set?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: Building the set is O(n), and each number is visited at most twice overall (once to check if it's a start, once as part of walking its run), so the total work is O(n) despite the nested-looking while loop.

items.prompt_md: A sort-then-scan approach to longest-consecutive-sequence (sort the array, then count adjacent runs) has what time complexity, and how does it compare to the set-based approach?
options: O(n), the same as the set-based approach
O(n log n), strictly worse than the set-based approach's O(n)
O(n^2), strictly worse than the set-based approach's O(n)
O(log n), strictly better than the set-based approach's O(n)
items.answer: O(n log n), strictly worse than the set-based approach's O(n)
items.explanation_md: Sorting costs O(n log n) no matter how cheap the scan afterward is. The set-based trick trades that sort for a hash set, getting the whole job done in O(n).

items.prompt_md: Why does a `set` beat sorting when a problem only needs "does X exist" or "is this a duplicate" — and doesn't care about order?
options: Sorting is always incorrect for these problems
A set answers membership in O(1) average per check and never needs to look at the rest of the data, while sorting pays O(n log n) up front and also reorders the input
Sets use less memory than any array
Sorting only works on numbers, not strings
items.answer: A set answers membership in O(1) average per check and never needs to look at the rest of the data, while sorting pays O(n log n) up front and also reorders the input
items.explanation_md: When you don't need the data in order, sorting is doing unrequested work. A set gets the same yes/no answer in less total time and leaves the original array untouched.

items.prompt_md: For the "no two entries may collide" style constraint (e.g., no row, column, or 3x3 box may repeat a digit), what's the right way to use sets?
options: One giant set shared across all rows, columns, and boxes combined
One set per group (each row, each column, each box), inserting as you scan and rejecting on a hit
Sort each row and compare adjacent elements
A single dict mapping digit to its one allowed position
items.answer: One set per group (each row, each column, each box), inserting as you scan and rejecting on a hit
items.explanation_md: Each group's "no duplicates" constraint is independent, so each needs its own membership tracker. A shared set would falsely flag a digit that's fine in its own row but happens to appear in a different row too.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of the one-pass `set`-based contains-duplicate check, and why?
options: O(1), because sets don't store their contents
O(n) in the worst case, because every distinct value seen so far is stored in the set
O(n^2), because the set stores every pair of values
O(log n), because sets use a balanced tree internally
items.answer: O(n) in the worst case, because every distinct value seen so far is stored in the set
items.explanation_md: If the array has no duplicates at all, the set grows to hold all n values before the scan finishes — so the extra memory used is proportional to n.

items.prompt_md: A teammate writes `return len(set(nums)) != len(nums)` for contains-duplicate instead of an early-exit loop with `in`. How does this compare to the early-exit version?
options: It's a correctness bug — sets can't detect duplicates
Same idea and same O(n) time/space, but it always builds the full set even when a duplicate appears in the first two elements, so it can't short-circuit
It's asymptotically slower, O(n^2), because `len()` on a set is O(n)
It uses O(1) space because dedup happens in place
items.answer: Same idea and same O(n) time/space, but it always builds the full set even when a duplicate appears in the first two elements, so it can't short-circuit
items.explanation_md: Both express the identical membership idea and share the same worst-case complexity; the difference is only that the early-exit loop can return True the moment it finds a repeat, while `set(nums)` always processes every element first.

items.prompt_md: What distinguishes the set-membership pattern from the hash-map-lookup pattern (like Two Sum) when you're deciding which to reach for?
options: Set membership is only for integers; hash-map lookup is only for strings
In hash-map lookup you store a value because you'll need to report something else about it later (an index, a count); in set membership, existence alone is the entire answer
There is no real difference — they always produce identical code
Set membership requires the input to be pre-sorted; hash-map lookup does not
items.answer: In hash-map lookup you store a value because you'll need to report something else about it later (an index, a count); in set membership, existence alone is the entire answer
items.explanation_md: Two Sum needs to return an *index* once a complement is found — that requires a dict. Contains Duplicate only ever needs "have I seen this," so a set is the more honest tool.

items.prompt_md: You must determine whether any value in an array repeats, with no need to know its position or how many times. Which approach fits with a single pass and no sorting?
options: Sort the array, then check adjacent elements for equality
Keep a set of values seen so far and check membership before inserting each new value
Build a prefix count array and query ranges in O(1)
Keep a hash map of values to their indices and return the index pair on a match
items.answer: Keep a set of values seen so far and check membership before inserting each new value
items.explanation_md: "Has this been seen before, yes or no" with nothing else to report is the fingerprint for set membership — no paired value is needed, so a set is the leaner and clearer tool than a dict.

items.prompt_md: Given an unsorted array of integers, you need the length of the longest run of consecutive integers (e.g. `[100, 4, 200, 1, 3, 2]` → 4, for `1,2,3,4`), and you must do it faster than sorting allows. Which approach fits?
options: Sort the array and scan for the longest run of adjacent, increasing-by-one values
Put every value in a hash set, then for each value that has no predecessor in the set, walk forward counting the run
Use two pointers starting from each end of the array
Build a prefix sum array and binary search for run boundaries
items.answer: Put every value in a hash set, then for each value that has no predecessor in the set, walk forward counting the run
items.explanation_md: Needing better-than-O(n log n) on an unsorted array is the tell: a set gives O(1) predecessor checks, and only starting counts at true run-boundaries keeps the whole scan O(n).

items.prompt_md: You need to remove duplicate elements from a list of usernames, and the final order doesn't matter. Which approach is both correct and simplest?
options: Nested loops comparing every pair of usernames and deleting later matches
Convert the list to a set and back to a list
Sort the list, then merge adjacent duplicates with a two-pointer sweep
Build a dict mapping username to its first index and reconstruct order manually
items.answer: Convert the list to a set and back to a list
items.explanation_md: When order is irrelevant and you just need uniqueness, a set does the dedup job directly in O(n) — no sort, no manual index bookkeeping needed.

items.prompt_md: You're validating a Sudoku board: every row, every column, and every 3x3 box must independently contain no repeated digit. What's the right way to check this in one pass over the board?
options: Sort every row, column, and box and check for adjacent equal digits
Use one shared set for the whole board and reject any digit seen twice anywhere
Keep a separate set per row, per column, and per box; check membership then insert as you scan each cell once
Use nested loops to compare every pair of cells on the board
items.answer: Keep a separate set per row, per column, and per box; check membership then insert as you scan each cell once
items.explanation_md: Each group's uniqueness constraint is independent, so each needs its own set. One shared set (or a global sort) would confuse a digit repeating across different groups with a real violation.

items.prompt_md: You need to check whether two arrays of the same length contain exactly the same set of distinct values, ignoring how many times each appears or in what order. Which approach fits?
options: Sort both arrays and compare element by element
Convert both arrays to sets and compare the sets for equality
Use two pointers walking both arrays simultaneously
Build a prefix sum for each array and compare the totals
items.answer: Convert both arrays to sets and compare the sets for equality
items.explanation_md: "Same distinct values, order and count irrelevant" is exactly what set equality tests, in O(n) — sorting would spend effort on an ordering nobody asked for.

items.prompt_md: Given two arrays, you need to find the values that appear in both, with each shared value reported only once. Which approach fits?
options: Nested loops checking every element of one array against every element of the other
Sort both arrays and merge them like the merge step of merge sort
Put one array's values into a set, then scan the other array checking membership in that set
Build a prefix sum array from the first array
items.answer: Put one array's values into a set, then scan the other array checking membership in that set
items.explanation_md: "Is this value also present over there" is a membership question — a set turns each check into O(1), giving O(n + m) total instead of O(n·m) nested loops or O(n log n + m log m) for sorting.

items.prompt_md: You're given a huge unsorted array and asked only: "is the value 42 present anywhere in this array?" — a single query, checked once. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Build a full hash set of the whole array just to check one value, then discard it
A single linear scan with early exit the moment 42 is found, no extra structure
Sort the entire array first, then binary search for 42
Use two pointers converging from both ends
items.answer: A single linear scan with early exit the moment 42 is found, no extra structure
items.explanation_md: Set membership pays off when you check *many* values against the same collection repeatedly. For a single one-off query, a plain O(n) scan with early exit does the same job without the overhead of building a structure you'll only query once.

items.prompt_md: You must determine, for an array of n integers, whether it's possible to pick two *different* indices i and j such that `nums[i] == nums[j]`. You don't need to know which indices. Which approach fits?
options: Sort the array in O(n log n), then check every adjacent pair for equality
Insert values into a set one at a time; if an insertion finds the value already present, return true immediately
Use a nested loop comparing `nums[i]` to `nums[j]` for every i != j
Build a prefix sum array and check if any prefix sum repeats
items.answer: Insert values into a set one at a time; if an insertion finds the value already present, return true immediately
items.explanation_md: This is the contains-duplicate question restated: no index is needed, so a set with an O(1) average membership check per element beats both the O(n log n) sort and the O(n^2) nested loop.

items.prompt_md: You need to count, for every distinct word in a document, exactly how many times it occurs. Which approach fits best?
options: A set of words, since you only need to know which words exist
A hash map from word to running count, incremented on each occurrence
Sort all the words and binary search for each one's boundaries
Two pointers scanning the document from both ends
items.answer: A hash map from word to running count, incremented on each occurrence
items.explanation_md: This needs a value attached to each key (the running count), not just existence — that's a hash map's job, not a set's. Recognizing when membership alone is *not* enough is as important as recognizing when it is.

items.prompt_md: An array of n integers is unsorted, and you need the length of the longest streak of consecutive values in O(n) time — not O(n log n). A teammate proposes starting a forward-counting walk from *every* element in the array, not just from run-starts. What's wrong with that plan, and what should be done instead?
options: Nothing is wrong; it's already O(n)
It's correct but wastes space; switch to a dict instead of a set
It can degrade to O(n^2), because elements in the middle of a long run get re-walked repeatedly; only start a walk from values whose predecessor is absent from the set
It's incorrect and will miss valid runs; sorting is required instead
items.answer: It can degrade to O(n^2), because elements in the middle of a long run get re-walked repeatedly; only start a walk from values whose predecessor is absent from the set
items.explanation_md: Starting from every element re-scans the same run once per member of that run. Gating each start on "predecessor not in the set" ensures each run is walked exactly once, restoring the O(n) bound.

items.prompt_md: You're given an array and need to know only whether *any* value appears more than once — true or false, nothing more, and you must not reorder the caller's array. Which approach fits?
options: Sort a copy of the array in place, disturbing the original's order in your local copy, then scan for adjacent equal values
Build a hash set while scanning the original array in its given order, checking membership before each insert
Use two pointers, one from the front and one from the back, comparing values
Build a prefix sum array and look for repeated prefix totals
items.answer: Build a hash set while scanning the original array in its given order, checking membership before each insert
items.explanation_md: A set-based scan never needs to touch or reorder the input at all, and settles the yes/no question in one O(n) pass — exactly what "don't disturb the array, just answer duplicate-or-not" calls for.

items.prompt_md: Two lists of usernames may contain duplicates within themselves. You need to know how many *distinct* usernames appear in the first list but not in the second at all. Which approach fits?
options: Sort both lists and merge-walk them looking for gaps
Convert both lists to sets, then compute the set difference of the first set minus the second
Nested loops checking every name in list one against every name in list two
Build a prefix sum array over list one's length
items.answer: Convert both lists to sets, then compute the set difference of the first set minus the second
items.explanation_md: "Distinct values in A absent from B" is precisely set difference — converting to sets both dedups and gives O(1) average membership checks, all in O(n + m) time.
